nodes.label: Articles
nodes.description: Dutch has two definite articles: 'de' (common gender) and 'het' (neuter). One indefinite: 'een'.
nodes.english_bridge: Like 'the' in English, but Dutch splits it into de/het based on noun gender.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Unlike Chinese (no articles at all), every Dutch noun requires an article. This is one of the biggest adjustments.

nodes.label: de vs het
nodes.description: 'de' is used for common gender nouns (masculine + feminine merged). 'het' is for neuter nouns. Roughly 75% of nouns are de-words.
nodes.english_bridge: English lost grammatical gender centuries ago. Dutch still has it, but simplified to two classes.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese has no grammatical gender. Memorizing de/het is a new cognitive category.

nodes.label: een (indefinite)
nodes.description: 'een' (unstressed: 'n) is the indefinite article for ALL nouns regardless of gender. Like English 'a/an'.
nodes.english_bridge: Exactly like English 'a/an' — one form for all nouns.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Somewhat like using 一 (yī) before a measure word, but 'een' is used much more broadly.

nodes.label: Noun Gender
nodes.description: Dutch nouns are either common gender (de) or neuter (het). Gender affects articles, adjective endings, and pronouns.
nodes.english_bridge: English has no noun gender. Dutch gender must largely be memorized with each noun.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese has no noun gender either. Learn each noun with its article: 'de tafel', not just 'tafel'.

nodes.label: Nouns
nodes.description: Dutch nouns are capitalized only when proper nouns. They inflect for plural and have grammatical gender.
nodes.english_bridge: Similar to English nouns but with gender and different plural patterns.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Unlike Chinese, Dutch nouns change form for plural (no measure words needed).

nodes.label: Plural Forms
nodes.description: Main plural suffixes: -en (most nouns), -s (nouns ending in unstressed -el, -em, -en, -er, -je), -eren (irregular: kind→kinderen).
nodes.english_bridge: English only has -s/-es. Dutch has -en as the main plural, which can cause vowel changes.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese plurals are context-dependent or use 们. Dutch always marks plural on the noun itself.

nodes.label: Diminutives (-je)
nodes.description: Adding -je (and variants: -tje, -etje, -pje, -kje) makes a diminutive. ALL diminutives are het-words.
nodes.english_bridge: English rarely uses diminutives (booklet, droplet). Dutch uses them constantly in everyday speech.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Similar to 小 (xiǎo) prefix in Chinese, but as a suffix and grammatically obligatory for het-gender.

nodes.label: Compound Nouns
nodes.description: Dutch freely compounds nouns. The last element determines the gender. Written as one word.
nodes.english_bridge: English uses spaces between noun compounds. Dutch smashes them together: 'voetbalwedstrijd'.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Similar to Chinese compounding (足球比赛→voetbalwedstrijd), but written as one word in Dutch.

nodes.label: Pronouns
nodes.description: Dutch pronouns have full and reduced (unstressed) forms. The distinction matters in speech.
nodes.english_bridge: Similar system to English (I/me, he/him) but Dutch has extra reduced forms.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese pronouns don't change form (我 is always 我). Dutch pronouns shift by stress and function.

nodes.label: Personal Pronouns
nodes.description: ik (I), jij/je (you sg.), hij (he), zij/ze (she), het (it), wij/we (we), jullie (you pl.), zij/ze (they).
nodes.english_bridge: Maps closely to English I/you/he/she/it/we/you/they. Main difference: jij vs je (stressed vs unstressed).
nodes.chinese_bridge: 我/你/他/她/它/我们/你们/他们 — same conceptual structure, but Dutch has stressed/unstressed variants.

nodes.label: Object Pronouns
nodes.description: mij/me (me), jou/je (you), hem (him), haar (her), het (it), ons (us), jullie (you pl.), hen/hun/ze (them).
nodes.english_bridge: Like English me/you/him/her/it/us/them. Dutch hem/haar follow grammatical gender.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese uses the same form for subject and object. Dutch changes form: ik→mij, hij→hem.

nodes.label: Possessive Pronouns
nodes.description: mijn (my), jouw/je (your), zijn (his), haar (her), ons/onze (our), jullie (your pl.), hun (their).
nodes.english_bridge: Direct parallel to English my/your/his/her/our/their. 'Ons/onze' splits based on de/het noun.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Like 我的/你的/他的 but without the 的 particle — possessives directly precede the noun.

nodes.label: Verbs
nodes.description: Dutch verbs conjugate for person/number in present tense. The infinitive ends in -en. Stem is the base form.
nodes.english_bridge: More conjugation than English (English only adds -s for third person singular).
nodes.chinese_bridge: Unlike Chinese (verbs don't change), Dutch verbs conjugate. This requires learning conjugation patterns.

nodes.label: Infinitive
nodes.description: The base form of Dutch verbs ends in -en (sometimes -n). Used after modal verbs and 'te'.
nodes.english_bridge: Like English 'to work' but Dutch infinitives are just 'werken' (no 'to' needed after modals).
nodes.chinese_bridge: Like the base form of Chinese verbs (工作). No 'to' equivalent needed in Dutch after modal verbs.
examples: Ik wil werken. (I want to work)

nodes.label: Stem Rule
nodes.description: The verb stem = infinitive minus -en. If last letter of stem is in 't kofschip', past tense adds -te/-ten; otherwise -de/-den.
nodes.english_bridge: English has strong/weak verbs (walk→walked, sing→sang). Dutch has a similar but more systematic rule.
nodes.chinese_bridge: No parallel in Chinese — this is a uniquely Germanic feature requiring practice.

nodes.label: Present Tense
nodes.description: ik + stem, jij/hij/zij/het + stem+t (but NO -t if jij follows the verb in inversion), wij/jullie/zij + infinitive.
nodes.english_bridge: English only changes for 3rd sg (he works). Dutch changes for all persons.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese verbs never change form. Each Dutch person/number combination has its own ending.

nodes.label: Inversion -t Drop
nodes.description: When 'jij/je' follows the verb (in questions or inverted sentences), the verb loses its -t ending.
nodes.english_bridge: No English parallel — English doesn't change verb form in questions.
nodes.chinese_bridge: No parallel in Chinese. This is a specific Dutch quirk to memorize.

nodes.label: Separable Verbs
nodes.description: Verbs with prefixes that split off in main clauses: the prefix goes to the END of the sentence.
nodes.english_bridge: Similar to English phrasal verbs (turn off → I turn the light off). Dutch is more systematic.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Similar to Chinese verb-complement structures (打开→打…开) but the prefix travels to sentence end.
examples: opstaan (to get up): Ik sta om 7 uur op.

nodes.label: Modal Verbs
nodes.description: kunnen (can), mogen (may), moeten (must), willen (want), zullen (shall/will), hoeven (need to — mostly negative).
nodes.english_bridge: Direct parallel to English can/may/must/want/will. Modal + infinitive at end of clause.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Like 能/可以/必须/想要/会 — modal precedes the main verb. Word order differs in subordinate clauses.

nodes.label: Simple Past (Imperfect)
nodes.description: Weak verbs: stem + te/de (singular) or ten/den (plural). Strong verbs: vowel change (irregular). Used mainly in writing/narrative.
nodes.english_bridge: Like English walked/ran. Dutch uses imperfect mainly in written/formal Dutch; spoken Dutch prefers perfect tense.
nodes.chinese_bridge: No direct parallel — Chinese uses time adverbs (昨天) not verb changes. Dutch changes the verb itself.

nodes.label: Perfect Tense (Voltooid)
nodes.description: hebben/zijn + past participle. This is the default past tense in SPOKEN Dutch. Choice of hebben vs zijn is key.
nodes.english_bridge: Like English 'I have worked' but Dutch uses it where English would say 'I worked' in conversation.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Somewhat like 我已经工作了 (already completed), but Dutch uses it for all past events in speech.

nodes.label: Past Participle (ge-...)
nodes.description: Regular: ge + stem + t/d. Strong: ge + changed vowel stem + en. No ge- prefix if verb starts with be-, ge-, er-, her-, ont-, ver-.
nodes.english_bridge: Like English worked/gone/seen. Dutch adds ge- prefix which English lost.
nodes.chinese_bridge: No parallel — Chinese verbs don't change for tense. The ge- prefix is a purely Dutch/Germanic feature.

nodes.label: hebben vs zijn (aux)
nodes.description: Most verbs use 'hebben'. Verbs of motion/change-of-state use 'zijn': gaan, komen, rijden, worden, blijven, zijn, etc.
nodes.english_bridge: English always uses 'have'. Dutch splits — motion/state-change verbs use 'zijn' (like French/Italian).
nodes.chinese_bridge: No parallel in Chinese (no auxiliary for past tense). This distinction must be memorized per verb.

nodes.label: zijn & hebben (main verbs)
nodes.description: 'zijn' = to be (highly irregular). 'hebben' = to have. Both are essential and irregular.
nodes.english_bridge: zijn = to be (ik ben, jij bent, hij is, wij zijn). hebben = to have (ik heb, jij hebt, hij heeft).
nodes.chinese_bridge: 是 (shì) ≈ zijn for identity/classification. 有 (yǒu) ≈ hebben. But Dutch 'zijn' is used much more broadly.

nodes.label: Word Order
nodes.description: Dutch word order is governed by the V2 rule in main clauses, and verb-final in subordinate clauses.
nodes.english_bridge: English word order is fairly fixed SVO. Dutch is more flexible but with strict rules about verb position.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese is SVO like English. Dutch has V2 which feels unusual — the verb must be second, whatever comes first.
examples: Ik werk thuis. / Thuis werk ik. (not: Thuis ik werk)

nodes.label: V2 Rule (Verb Second)
nodes.description: In Dutch main clauses, the finite verb ALWAYS occupies the second position, regardless of what comes first.
nodes.english_bridge: English has this only in questions ('Are you coming?'). Dutch applies it to all statements when anything non-subject comes first.
nodes.chinese_bridge: No equivalent in Chinese. This is the most fundamental Dutch word order rule and takes time to internalize.

nodes.label: Subject-Verb Inversion
nodes.description: When anything other than the subject starts the sentence, subject and verb invert (V2 consequence).
nodes.english_bridge: English only inverts in questions. Dutch inverts whenever a non-subject element is fronted.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese uses topic-comment structure instead. Dutch inversion is the equivalent mechanism.
examples: Ik ga morgen. → Morgen ga ik.
In de klas zitten twintig studenten.

nodes.label: Subordinate Clauses (SOV)
nodes.description: In subordinate clauses (after conjunctions like dat, omdat, als, toen), the verb moves to the END.
nodes.english_bridge: English keeps SVO in all clauses. Dutch flips to SOV in subordinate clauses — a major difference.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese subordinate clauses also tend toward SOV-like patterns. This might feel more natural for Chinese speakers.
examples: Ik weet dat hij morgen komt.
Ze gaat niet omdat ze ziek is.

nodes.label: Time-Manner-Place Order
nodes.description: Adverbials follow the order: Time first, then Manner, then Place. Opposite of English (Place-Manner-Time).
nodes.english_bridge: English: 'I go to Amsterdam by train tomorrow' (Place-Manner-Time). Dutch reverses: 'Ik ga morgen met de trein naar Amsterdam'.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese also uses Time before Place (我明天去阿姆斯特丹). Dutch is closer to Chinese in this respect.
examples: Ik ga morgen (T) met de trein (M) naar Amsterdam (P).

nodes.label: Adjectives
nodes.description: Dutch adjectives inflect (add -e) when used attributively before a noun. Predicative adjectives are uninflected.
nodes.english_bridge: English adjectives never change. Dutch adjectives add -e in most attributive positions.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese adjectives use 的 (de) as a structural particle. Dutch uses -e inflection instead.
examples: groot (big) → een grote man / de grote man / De man is groot.

nodes.label: Adjective Inflection Rules
nodes.description: Add -e EXCEPT: indefinite article + het-word (een groot huis). Rule: 'een + het-word = no -e'.
nodes.english_bridge: No English parallel. The 'een groot huis' exception is the hardest part.
nodes.chinese_bridge: No parallel in Chinese. Remember the exception: een + neuter noun = no -e on adjective.

nodes.label: Comparative (-er)
nodes.description: Add -er to form comparatives. 'dan' = than. Irregular: goed→beter, veel→meer, weinig→minder.
nodes.english_bridge: Like English -er (bigger, faster). Same irregulars: good→better, much→more.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese uses 比 (bǐ) for comparison. Dutch changes the adjective form instead.

nodes.label: Superlative (-st)
nodes.description: Add -st (or -est) to form superlatives. Used with 'de' or 'het'. Irregular: goed→best, veel→meest.
nodes.english_bridge: Like English -est (biggest, fastest). Same irregulars: good→best, many→most.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese uses 最 (zuì). Dutch changes the adjective: groot→grootst.

nodes.label: Negation (niet / geen)
nodes.description: 'niet' negates verbs/adjectives/adverbs. 'geen' negates nouns (replaces een/no article). Position of 'niet' varies.
nodes.english_bridge: English uses 'not' for everything. Dutch splits: 'geen' replaces articles before nouns, 'niet' for everything else.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese uses 不 (bù) for most negation, 没 (méi) for 'not have'. Dutch geen/niet split is somewhat similar.

nodes.label: Questions
nodes.description: Yes/no questions: invert subject and verb. Wh-questions: question word + V2 + subject.
nodes.english_bridge: Yes/no questions invert like English. Wh-questions: question word is position 1, verb is position 2.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese uses 吗 for yes/no questions (no inversion). Dutch inversion is a new pattern to learn.

nodes.label: Question Words
nodes.description: wie (who), wat (what), waar (where), wanneer (when), hoe (how), waarom (why), welke/welk (which), hoeveel (how many).
nodes.english_bridge: Direct one-to-one mapping with English who/what/where/when/how/why/which/how many.
nodes.chinese_bridge: 谁/什么/哪里/什么时候/怎么/为什么/哪个/多少 — exact conceptual parallels.

nodes.label: Prepositions
nodes.description: Common: in, op, aan, bij, van, naar, met, voor, na, over, onder, tussen, door.
nodes.english_bridge: Most have English equivalents but usage differs. 'op' can mean 'on' or 'at' depending on context.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Like Chinese prepositions (介词) but position differs: Dutch prepositions precede nouns like English.

nodes.label: Coordinating Conjunctions
nodes.description: en (and), maar (but), of (or), want (because — keep SVO), dus (so/therefore). These do NOT change word order.
nodes.english_bridge: and/but/or/because/so — exact parallels. Key: 'want' keeps normal word order (unlike 'omdat').
nodes.chinese_bridge: 和/但是/或者/因为/所以 — same conjunctions. The word-order effect (or lack thereof) is the tricky part.
examples: Ik kom niet want ik ben ziek.
Ze is moe dus ze gaat slapen.

nodes.label: Subordinating Conjunctions
nodes.description: dat (that), omdat (because), als (if/when), toen (when-past), terwijl (while), hoewel (although), zodat (so that). Push verb to END.
nodes.english_bridge: that/because/if/when/while/although — but these trigger verb-final word order in Dutch.
nodes.chinese_bridge: 因为/如果/当/虽然 — same concepts, but Dutch requires all verbs to cluster at the end of the clause.
examples: Ze blijft thuis omdat ze ziek is.
Als het regent, blijf ik binnen.

nodes.label: Numbers & Counting
nodes.description: 0-20 mostly irregular. Compound numbers: 21=eenentwintig (one-and-twenty). Inverted from English!
nodes.english_bridge: English: twenty-one. Dutch: eenentwintig (one-and-twenty). The ones digit comes FIRST in Dutch.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese: 二十一 (20+1). Dutch: eenentwintig (1+20). Dutch order is inverted from both Chinese and English.
examples: een, twee, drie... tien, elf, twaalf

nodes.label: Time Expressions
nodes.description: Days, months, telling time. Dutch clock: half drie = 2:30 (half before 3, not half past 2!).
nodes.english_bridge: Half past two (EN) = half drie (NL — half before three). This is a common mistake for English speakers.
nodes.chinese_bridge: Chinese 两点半 (2.5 hours) matches English half past 2. Dutch half drie is counterintuitive for both.
